My longtime relationship with antiperspirants was like that of a social smoker and cigarettes: I knew they were ultimately harmful and comforted myself with the notion that I would one day just. quit.   Then I got diagnosed with hypothyroidism and discovered a whole new level of hell, and it made me realize that the things I'd always taken for granted about myself weren't so great after all.           Edit: typos
I hate to be this guy, but this doesn't seem like something you should be ashamed of. I have hypothyroidism and it's not uncommon for me to have a little bit of sweat on me. It's not a deal breaker.   I hope you feel better!
Thank you! I don't really care if people know I have hypothyroidism - I just wish that I had known it earlier in life when I found out I was pregnant at 16, and I wish I had known that there was an alternative to the awful things that are antiperspirants (like the fact that they make us sweat...).
Oh my god, why does everything make us sweat?
Because we're humans and we sweat.

Hate group says the accused Florida school shooter did paramilitary exercises and got a rifle from members.

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) has accused the alleged shooter, Nikolas Cruz, of having "a history of anti-Semitic and racist statements" before he allegedly went on his rampage at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School in Parkland, Florida, on Wednesday.

The ADL said that Cruz, 19, was active in the group's Florida chapter and had posted about its activities on social media.

"Cruz is known to have posted anti-Semitic and racist messages on social media," the ADL said in a statement. "He also attended a meeting of the National Socialist Movement, a white supremacist group, in 2012."

"We are investigating this incident, and we will continue our efforts to prevent hate crimes against Jewish and Latino communities," ADL CEO Jonathan Greenblatt said in an emailed statement. "We are deeply concerned that this tragic event is a result of the failure to adequately address the persistent threat posed by white nationalists in our society, especially during the Trump era."

Can I Run Python From Notepad?

A few days ago, I got a curious question from a reader:

Hey, I have a program that reads text from the internet and writes it to a file. The text is in a text format and I need to run it on a computer. Are there any programs that will read text from a notepad and then save it as a python file?

The program responds by asking:

No, because you would need to read the text in a text editor before running it.

However, I want to be able to read the text in a text editor, and then run it on a computer. So I tried to find a way to do that with python, but all of the programs I found said they need to see an image/file of the text being run.

I couldn’t find anything that would work other than writing the text myself. So now I’ve been wondering whether the programs I found need to see an image/file of the text being run.

Everyone talks about the technical talent crunch in Boston (and other hubs). I've seen an increase in salaries, and I think it's fair to say that even when there are no openings for entry-level positions, top-notch software engineers are willing to move elsewhere to land whatever positions they can find.
I'm not saying that I am not looking for a job in Boston, but I have noticed that I am doing far better in the Bay area than I was in Boston.  I believe this is due to the fact that the Bay area has a very different set of requirements for jobs than Boston does.

CRAIG Joubert, the referee at the centre of the controversy over Scotland’s narrow Rugby World Cup quarter final loss to Australia last year, has revealed why he ran off the pitch at.

The incident, which left Scotland players in tears and led to accusations that he had been racially abused by Australia supporters, was sparked after the home side had scored a try in the final minute of the match.

Joubert was given a two-week ban but the Rugby Football Union appealed against the punishment and a disciplinary hearing found he should have been handed a one-game ban.

And Joubert has now explained his decision to run off the pitch.

Speaking on his BBC Radio 5 live show, Joubert said: “I’ve never seen anything like it.

“I was just so shocked by what had happened.

“I thought I was just going to go over to the line and talk to the three Australian guys who were trying to get the Australian fans off their backs.

“I didn’t know who the referee was at the time. I didn’t know who the opponent was. I was just so shocked but I had no option other than to leave the field of play.

It's easy to dump shortcuts, files and other miscellany on to your desktop for quick access or for convenience's sake, but before long you can be left with a forest of icons.

There are so many icons these days that it's almost impossible to know which ones you should keep and which ones you should delete.

This guide will aim to make your desktop cleaner and more efficient by helping you to identify and decide which icons to keep and which to delete.

We'll also look at how to make your desktop look better with the help of a few simple tweaks in the background.

The first step is to try and figure out which apps you use the most often.

If you have an extensive collection of apps then you may want to keep them all, but if you only need five or six of them, then you might want to sort them out into different folders or categories.

One option is to create a folder for each app and then put them in a separate folder.

Another way to organise your apps is to have one folder for general system functions, another for contacts, other for emails, etc.

Finally, if you have dozens of icons on your desktop you don't need then you could do with creating a folder dedicated to them.

Nicola Sturgeon has assured the family of Sheku Bayoh, the Sierra Leonean man who died on a pavement in Kirkcaldy, Fife, minutes after being detained by police in May, that she will.

In a statement released on Tuesday afternoon, the First Minister also called for a full investigation into the death of Mr Bayoh, who was detained by police in the city centre in the “dead of night” on his way to a job interview.

She said: “We are deeply saddened by the murder of Sheku Bayoh and our hearts go out to his family at this time.

“As the matter is now before the courts, I cannot comment further while the justice process is ongoing.

“I have spoken to the family and I understand they fully support the police inquiry into the death. We will continue to support them as we await the outcome of the trial.”

The statement comes after Mr Bayoh’s family revealed that a post-mortem examination showed he had been “stripped of his clothes, assaulted and restrained for two hours before he was placed in the back of the police van” and taken to St Andrew’s Police Station.

One of the most popular features on our website yesterday was a picture gallery of revellers photographed over the years at popular Portsmouth nightclubs.

The images, taken from across Portsmouth, were shared widely on social media.

Many of the images show clubbers at Club One, The Hub or the Stag and Horns Club.

Some of the pictures are even from before the club was renamed.

One of the best images that has been shared so far shows revellers in a group at Club One.

The image, which appears to have been taken in the early 2000s, was taken by one of our readers, who shared the stunning image on Facebook.

Another picture shows revellers at the Stag and Horns Club in 2011.

The image, which also appears to be taken around the same time as the shot below, is said to have been taken by a member of staff.

Another image captures revellers at the Stag, Horns and The Hub in 2012.

The image, taken by a member of staff at each venue, captures thousands of revellers gathered together within the club.

Today we examine the XFX Double Dissipation R9 280X video card, comparing it to a fiercely priced custom GeForce GTX 770.

The AMD Radeon R9 280X is the company's mid-range graphics card that will cost around $360 (£260), and is based on the same architecture as the Radeon HD 7870, but with a few extra features. This card is based on the R9 270X, but has a different cooler and cooler shroud compared to the R9 270X.

The card is also slightly bigger than the Radeon HD 7790, and the cooler seems to have a little more clearance. The card's PCB is slightly larger than the Radeon HD 7970, with the card's PCB being larger than its GPU (the GPU itself is just slightly smaller than the Radeon HD 7770).

As with the Radeon HD 7970, the card has the ability to support DirectX 11, but only in the form of a supersampling option - a feature only offered in the Radeon HD 7850, Radeon HD 7950, and Radeon HD 7970 (though it's possible that AMD will offer an adaptive shaders option as well for the R9 280X).

ZANESVILLE - The Zanesville Community Theatre will open its 56th season this weekend with the award-winning drama, "Of Mice and Men" by John Steinbeck.
This year marks the centennial of the beginning of the Great Depression in the United States. It's a time when many people were struggling to survive, and it was especially difficult for the poor, who were forced to work and make little money.
The play's main character, George, was an impoverished farmer who was forced to get by on scraps of food and cash when his wife died. He was determined not to be a burden to his children, so he refused to sell his crops or go to market, even though the family needed the money.
His neighbors joined him in refusing to sell anything to the rich, forcing the man to live off the land as they made their own bread.

It was poignant sitting opposite the man whose ambition it had been to remake capitalism for an age of austerity. As he spoke, I listened to his measured arguments and saw the logic in them. At the heart of his position was a commitment to change. He argued that by investing more than £100bn in public services, the government was taking an important step in addressing the country's social needs.

I felt as though the conversation had taken me back to another time. We were once a different country. We would not be so blase about a government spending a disproportionate amount of money on people who are poor, but who are also old and disabled or sick. The idea that those with good health could be left exposed to the virus seemed absurd.

It was a time when even the poorest in our country were expected to go to work. They were expected to do everything possible to make sure they could make enough money to get their families through the difficult years after the war.

I remember one of my teachers telling me that these people had seen their relatives killed in the war and that they were still expected to go to work every day.

Heritage proponents in Southampton are upset that Bruce County intends to sell the 124-year-old former St. Mary’s Anglican Church.

The county announced earlier this week it was selling the building at 26 Sussex Road for $1.5 million.

“It’s a complete surprise,” said Robert J. Van Horne, who has been working on preserving the church since 2013. “We have no idea why they’re doing this.”

Van Horne has been fighting to get grants to make repairs to the church and prevent demolition, which he said could result in as many as 80 jobs.

“We are not going to let this community down,” he said. “It would be a tremendous loss.”

St. Mary’s was constructed between 1886 and 1887 and served residents of the Southampton area until it was closed down in 1980. It was then bought by the Roman Catholic Diocese of Kingston and reopened in 2005.

After the diocese closed St. Mary’s in 2008, Southampton resident Mike Whelan and his wife, Judy, started a fund-raising campaign to save the church.

Cloudflare presents a primer on "anti-patterns" that have transformed IoT devices into ghastly security nightmares.

A new wave of smart home devices have emerged over the past few years, with the rise of connected TVs and smart appliances.

But while the devices are designed to make consumers' lives easier, they also make them much more vulnerable to hackers.

One recent example came in the form of the Equifax data breach in 2017, when hackers stole the personal information from 143 million Americans – including names, Social Security numbers, birthdates and address.

The hack exposed the information of an estimated 145 million customers in the UK and Ireland as well.

According to a report last week by the Ponemon Institute, "the cost of data breaches in 2018 will be $1.6 trillion, up 6% over 2017."

And while the cost may seem steep, there's no reason why consumers shouldn't be concerned about it.

In fact, one of the most startling findings of the Ponemon study is that many companies are not doing enough to prevent data breaches.

Israel Air Force is due to receive F-35 stealth fighters from manufacturer Lockheed Martin beginning in late 2015.

The program is already on schedule and there is no need for a delay, the Prime Minister’s office said Wednesday when asked about the latest reports that the Israeli air force has been asked to cancel its order.

“The Israeli Air Force is committed to the project,” the statement said.

The source reportedly declined to comment further on the matter, saying that the talks were ongoing.

On Wednesday morning, Israel Radio reported that the new deal could still be reached and that the Israeli air force was expected to sign the deal with Lockheed Martin sometime this week.

In late February, the Israeli military agreed to purchase 36 of the planes as part of a $3.2 billion contract.

The Peoria Rivermen came back to tie the game twice against the Rockford IceHogs but fell 4-2 before 5,521at the MetroCentre Friday night.

After a scoreless first period, the IceHogs scored three times in the second and fourth periods to secure the victory.

Rockford (10-12-0) led 2-0 after two periods, but Peoria (11-13-1) tied it at 2-2 when Justin Williams scored his fifth goal of the season with 5:01 to play in the third.

Rockford responded with three straight goals to take a 4-2 lead with less than four minutes remaining in regulation.

Pekka Luukkonen made 20 saves for Peoria, while Jeremy Helvig stopped 25 shots for Rockford.

Friday's game was the second of a three-game series between the Rivermen and IceHogs. The Rivermen return to action Saturday afternoon when they host the IceHogs at the MetroCentre at 3 p.m.

Saturday's game will be the third of a four-game homestand for the Rivermen. Peoria will begin a 10-game road trip on Wednesday against the Iowa Wild at 7:05 p.m.

Digital consumers are being bombarded with one kind of messaging or another.

In fact, there’s a flood of messaging options out there — and they’re all trying to tell you something.

With so many messages vying for your attention, it’s hard to tell which ones are important and which ones are spam.

So I took my two favorite apps, MailChimp (a free email marketing service) and Constant Contact (a paid email marketing service), and sorted them by the number of messages that they send over a given time period.

I found that both services are sending me a lot of messages, but I don’t want to read all of them. And I don’t want to spend lots of time reading through all of them.

What I want to do is filter out the messages that are really important, and ignore all the other messages.

And those are the messages that you’ll find here.

You can see how MailChimp and Constant Contact stack up on this simple metric:

The top three messages I’m seeing from MailChimp are usually emails from people requesting “free stuff,” and some of the other messages don’t even make my list.

Warning shot to the City as trader Tom Hayes’ conviction over Libor scandal upheld | City A.M.

The City of London has launched a fresh warning shot at Barclays and others in respect of Britain’s financial crisis after a judge confirmed a jury’s conviction of Tom Hayes, a trader who was accused of trading with false documents.

Hayes was found guilty by a jury last year of conspiracy to defraud a number of banks, including Barclays and HSBC, out of around £450m (€510m).

Ahead of his sentencing on Monday, Hayes’ lawyer, Richard Smith, said that the verdict would affect the City’s appetite for risk.

“If we are not confident of justice being served, it will weigh on the City’s willingness to engage with people in the course of doing business,” he said.

“There is no evidence as yet that there is an appetite for risk in the City but it may well be that some companies will be more reluctant to take risks when there is a possibility that they could be caught up in an investigation.”

Smith said the City was “very concerned” by the outcome of the case, which had been followed by a long-running investigation into the manipulation of Libor, or the London interbank offered rate, by Barclays, HSBC and other big banks.

Following Hurricane Sandy, members of the New York startup community have extended offers of help fellow entrepreneurs and other residents of the tri-state area caught without power and Internet by the storm. The offer includes food, free WiFi, information on power companies, and more.

“Everyone wants to help, so I hope they’ll take advantage of it,” said Todd Siegel, a cofounder of New York City startup accelerator Y Combinator.

Last year, Y Combinator ran a similar effort through its partner the Red Cross called “Give a Little.”

Siegel said the initial response has been “great.” He said he received several dozen emails from people in the New York area offering to help, with the largest contribution coming from a woman in Westchester County who was offering pizza for anyone who needed it.

“It’s great and inspiring to see so many people come together,” he said.

Siegel said he expects some people will simply take food instead of cash.

“We want to be able to say we want to give food, but it has to be worth it,” he said.

The visual for the track features the rapper standing in front of a wall of luxury cars, and behind him is a car with a custom paint job. As the camera follows Bada$$ and the cars, we see the rapper driving through the desert, hitting several different scenes including a street fight, a party and some street art.

Bada$$ has also shared a preview of his upcoming mixtape, "Narcos."

Check out the video for "Temptations" below:

This is the first time Bada$$ has shared footage from his upcoming project with fans, but he has teased it before. Back in October, he shared a teaser video for an upcoming song called "Thinking About You," which featured fellow rappers Ty Dolla $ign and Rico Nasty. He also teased his mixtape back in July with a snippet of an unreleased song called "Hustle."

He also recently previewed his new single "Savage Love." In the video, the rapper takes part in some sort of performance, but it isn't clear what exactly he's playing.

Tottenham Hotspur striker Son Heung-min was overwhelmingly chosen as the athlete of the year among Korean sportspeople.

Kang-ho Song also picked up a gold medal for rhythmic gymnastics on Wednesday, while Korean swimmer Choi Min-jeong won a bronze in the women’s 50m freestyle.

Heung-min was named the best footballer on Tuesday while Song was named the best rhythmic gymnast.

The winner of the Korean athlete of the year award will receive a total prize package of £5,000 ($6,000), including a £250 ($338) cash prize and a place at the prestigious IAAF World Championships next year. The winners of the other four awards will each receive £2,500 ($3,000).

The awards ceremony took place at the International Olympic Committee headquarters in Lausanne, Switzerland.

In the pantheon of Santa Barbara artists, perhaps no star shines brighter than Ray Strong (1905-2006).

Born in Santa Barbara in 1905, Strong was a man of many talents. His first love was drawing and art, but as he grew older he became an accomplished sculptor who was particularly noted for his bronze relief work.

He is best known for his sculptures of angels and saints that are displayed throughout the world, mostly in churches and churches affiliated with the Catholic Church.

One of his most impressive works is the statue of the Virgin Mary and Child, which is placed in the doorway of the Santa Barbara Mission. The angel is holding the child in her arms, while the child’s face is shown above them.

The Santa Barbara Mission is located at 1851 Mission Plaza, Suite B, in downtown Santa Barbara.

The Facebook data privacy and Russian election interference scandals show that artificial intelligence is still not up to many critical jobs in the technology sector.

Artificial intelligence (AI) has been used in numerous applications to help computers learn from experience, improve their decision-making, and even replace human workers. Many AI companies are focusing on these very specific applications, such as driving cars, making life-saving medical decisions, or helping humans make more money.

For the most part, these applications require less training than many of the other ways AI is being used. But they don't have a job market like other technologies, where workers who can't move easily into new roles often get stuck in the same one for several years.

"In many cases," said Andrew Ng, CEO of Alibaba's AI unit, "the training is simple, but the deployment is challenging."

Even for very basic tasks, like playing games, AI systems often require a large amount of data and training data before they can be taught to perform well. The more data required, the longer it will take for the system to improve. Some companies have tried to speed up this training process by using data from other people's computers, but this strategy is difficult to scale.

A small town in Ontario, Canada, has prompted fresh scrutiny of the bottled-water industry after its attempt secure a long-term water supply through the purchase of a well was outbid by the.

Canada: What is your view of the bottled water industry?.

The government of New Brunswick has announced that it has awarded a contract to a local company to supply water to all municipalities in the province, including a local hospital.

The city of Montreal, Quebec, has been struggling to obtain municipal water from its private well. And in Montreal’s west end, the water crisis is getting so bad that residents are having to drink their tap water.

PricewaterhouseCoopers, a professional services company, warned last month that the bottled water industry is more vulnerable than any other sector to changes in consumer habits.

Bottled-water sales were on the rise for the first time in five years in 2017, driven by a strong economy and consumers’ desires to be hydrated, according to PWC.

A new study by the U.S. government concluded that bottled water is not safer or healthier than tap water.

Despite the warnings, the bottled-water industry is expected to grow at a double-digit pace over the next 30 years.

The 3-year-old boy squirmed in his mother’s arms, an IV drip unspooling from his wrist, his skin flush with a pale pink rash.

He was born with a rare genetic disorder that prevents the body’s immune system from detecting and fighting the coronavirus, and the hospital’s doctors say his condition requires frequent visits from a respirator and constant care.

Now, his parents are pleading with South Korea to allow them to take him home to North Dakota — a demand they say has been stymied by an unexpected loophole in the country’s coronavirus lockdown.

They are “heartbroken” over their son’s fate, said Kristy Johnson, who traveled from Fargo, North Dakota, to Seoul earlier this month with her husband, Ryan, and other family members.

On April 15, they received a call from the local hospital informing them they were required to stay at home for the next two weeks, unless officials extended the period.

The family had hoped to be able to fly back home on April 25, but after the new deadline passed, the Johnsons were informed they would need to wait a week.

The last few years have proved challenging for offshore drilling major Transocean, which saw its day rates and contracting activity post significant declines.

However, the company’s latest results show that despite the tough market conditions it managed to improve its performance in the second half of the year.

Transocean reported a net loss attributable to shareholders of $1.6bn (£1.29bn), compared with a net loss of $919m in the same period last year.

Revenue fell by 6 per cent to $4.42bn, but this was still up from the $4.28bn achieved in the second half of 2016.

Operating costs decreased by 9 per cent to $2.94bn, while a 1 per cent increase in revenues resulted in a 13 per cent reduction in operating costs.

The company’s shares rose by 10 per cent following the results, closing at $20.68.

Transocean was also able to reduce its debt by $1.8bn during the first six months of 2017, bringing its total debt to $4.3bn.

A study led by researcher Lisa Kellogg of William & Mary's Virginia Institute of Marine Science shows that a restored oyster reef can remove up to 10 times more nitrogen from Chesapeake.

The research team's findings were published in the journal Ecological Applications.

"We're really excited about this study," said Kellogg. "This was the first time we analyzed data from a restoration project and discovered that oysters are not only the primary filter-bearers, but they also play a significant role in removing nutrients from the water."

Oysters are filter-bearers that collect sediment and organic matter from the water, sort it into different layers, and then break down the larger particles deeper in the water column. They also break down algae and other organisms.

The researchers found that oysters removed roughly 25 pounds per acre of sediment from Chesapeake Bay waters during the three-year period of the study, which began in 2010. That, combined with other improvements in water quality, could result in a nearly 50 percent reduction in nitrogen from Chesapeake.

"Nitrogen is the most important nutrient in Chesapeake Bay, and it gets into the water when nutrients in the soil and from fertilizers go through the water," said Kellogg. "

Some 190 Nigerian schoolgirls remain missing after being abducted last week, their head teacher has told the BBC - far more than the official figure.

Maimuna Abubakar, a teacher in Kagara town, told the BBC that she feared the number of girls missing would be much higher because the abductors had not released any video of their captives.

"I don't know if it is true or false but I have been told they have not released any video of the victims," she said.

It is not clear how many girls are still missing.

The government has said 276 girls were taken by the Islamic State group (IS) when it invaded the town on 20 December.

They were released by the militants last week after being held for around two weeks.

The release came amid growing international pressure on Nigeria to do more to stop the insurgents' attacks, particularly in the north-east of the country.

A presidential task force has been set up to tackle the problem.

Mr Jonathan's spokesman said a total of 187 girls had so far been found, and another 42 would be found this week.

Rabiah Hutchinson is used to being talked about. It’s not the most glamorous job, but it pays and she can do it from anywhere, anytime.

For the past decade, the 24-year-old has been traveling the world, working as a medical translator for Doctors Without Borders (MSF), providing direct care to patients in dangerous situations.

She is one of about 30 MSF translators working around the clock at a hospital in Syria right now, helping to evacuate patients from war-ravaged areas.

And on Tuesday, she was also helping to care for a baby girl who was born premature at the hospital.

Hutchinson was with her mother, Erika, and grandmother when she gave birth. She was in the delivery room only a few minutes before the child, Zaidah, was born.

The baby girl was born with a lung condition and will receive intensive care for weeks to come.

“I was very happy that the baby would be discharged today because she’s been here for about a week,” Hutchison said.

text_original:
Former President Bill Clinton speaks during a campaign stop for his wife Democratic presidential candidate Hilary Clinton, Jan. 19, 2016, in St. Paul, Minn.

A new book is set to hit shelves Monday titled "Hilary: The Woman Who Lost Her Way." It is the first book written about Hillary Clinton by a former reporter for The New York Times, Dan Balz.

In an interview, Balz said he was driven to write the book by a sense of urgency.

"I felt a real sense of urgency," Balz said. "This book had to come out, and it had to be done in this time frame. We needed to understand the forces that were at work in this election and what she might do if she were president."

Balz says he believes Hillary Clinton will have some support from journalists.

"I think she had a really difficult time with the press," Balz said. "She's going to need help, and I think that's why I wrote the book. I wanted to be able to help her."

Balz also pointed to a recent study showing that many journalists who covered Hillary Clinton found her to be unsympathetic and not very likable.

A number of Warriors players actively recruited DeMarcus Cousins to the Bay Area, but the newly-signed Warrior can definitely state which one of them presented the worst pitch.

Kanye West does it again.

The Chicago rapper, who has been in the midst of a divorce from Kim Kardashian for almost three years, is once again being sued by her mother Kris Jenner.

According to TMZ, Kris is suing Kanye for $40 million in unpaid child support.

The lawsuit claims Kim is owed around $3 million per month, or $40 million total.

“Kris Jenner is seeking a full and final payment of all of her obligations, including monthly payments for all of the children and all of their support obligations,” court documents read, according to TMZ.

“Kris has not received any of the monies owed to her from Kanye West since December 2013.”

Kim is also accused of violating terms of her prenup agreement, making it “impossible” for her to fully support herself.

Kris had given her daughter a prenup before she married Kanye in 2014, promising that she would get a slice of his fortune if he divorced her.

Although the laboriously negotiated and long-delayed Middle East "road map" received a diplomatic boost by the recent intervention of George W. Bush, the peace process has also been plagued by political intransigence at the highest echelons of the government, as well as from the Arab world itself.

Despite Bush's pro-peace stance, his administration and that of Prime Minister Ehud Olmert (who has been in office since January) have been unable to reach any agreement on key issues. This is particularly true in the realm of the Palestinian issue, where Olmert has repeatedly refused to release prisoners, while Bush has pushed for their release.

At the same time, the Palestinian leadership has continually balked at calls for negotiations, insisting that settlement construction and even the expansion of illegal settlements must continue apace.

In addition, Palestinian President Mahmoud Abbas has repeatedly voiced his opposition to any negotiated settlement of the conflict--including an offer of full Israeli withdrawal from all parts of the West Bank--as a prerequisite for any final agreement.

Indeed, Abbas openly declared during his visit to the United States this week that he would not accept any peace agreement unless Israel agreed to withdraw.

Minnesota State High School League has approved concussion insurance for student athletes starting this fall.

All Minnesota high school athletes will be required to have concussion insurance beginning with the 2015-16 school year. The cost of insurance is $10 per athlete per month.

The state health insurance plan’s Board of Directors approved the new policy at its monthly meeting on Friday, March 27. The board also voted to approve the use of $30,000 from Minnesota’s $2 million fund to help pay for the program.

Sports teams are required to have an insurance policy in place that covers injury and illness. But the state doesn’t require that all student athletes have insurance coverage. Those who don’t have insurance are allowed to participate in sports that aren’t part of a team, but parents are responsible for covering medical expenses.

State athletic directors say they have been working on this issue for years. Assistant Director of Health and Safety John Kostal says there is still room for improvement, saying he would like to see more information available and better education about the insurance requirements.

The Patently-O blog this week took a look at how the number of pending patent cases is changing. The number of patent applications filed in the US increased by 1.2% from last year, the most since 2007, according to the USPTO.

This year’s numbers also suggest that the number of patent applications being filed for the first time has declined since 2008, although it still remains at a high level.

The trend may be related to the current economic slowdown. Last year saw the highest number of patent applications filed in the US since 2001.

Some of the increase in applications could also reflect the fact that the USPTO will have to deal with an additional 10,000 patent applications a month from January 1st 2014.

The USPTO’s website shows that the number of pending patent applications increased to 6.5 million last year and is expected to rise further this year.

In addition to the increase in pending patent applications, the number of patent applications granted last year dropped by 0.9%, to 4.7 million.

The number of new patent applications filed in the US has fallen steadily since 2009, although it has still risen significantly from 2005.

The Seven Cs of Political Leadership!

Political Leadership is different than other leadership roles in that it requires you to make decisions and communicate with your team. This can be difficult because we often think of leaders as being charismatic and bold. We see them as having a clear vision for their organization, and they lead by example. In fact, the word leader comes from the Latin word for “teacher” and the Greek word for “leader”. When you think of a leader, you generally think of someone who is charismatic, bold, and has a clear vision of the goals, strategies and tactics of their organization.

This leads us to the next question: What is a leader?

A good leader is the type of person who inspires others to achieve the goals and strategies of his or her organization. A good leader is one who brings people together to accomplish a common goal. They are a visionary who can inspire others to achieve a common goal.

There are seven elements of a good leader and, in my opinion, these are the seven Cs of political leadership.

When it comes to safety in the workplace, organizational culture can be quite literally a matter of life and death.

The American Society for Training and Development (ASTD) advises employers that:

“Employers should take steps to create a safe and healthy workplace.”

While there are many other factors that contribute to workplace injuries, the ASTD believes that the most significant factor is organizational culture.

Organizational culture is the physical environment within which employees work.

What is the difference between organizational culture and an organizational structure?

Organizational culture is built on trust.

Trust is something that needs to be earned, and it can only be earned if you do everything in your power to ensure that those involved in your organization know that they have your full support.

You don’t get to trust people if they don’t have a positive working relationship with you.

On a fundamental level, organizational culture is a reflection of your company’s ability to create opportunities for its employees to thrive.

An organization’s culture can be an important indicator of its longevity, as well as its ability to deliver tangible results.

New Jersey lawmakers approved a plan to reinstate the individual mandate to try to stabilize the health insurance market.

The plan would require individuals to purchase coverage, including the cost of prescription drugs, if they earn more than $75,000 and have no dependents. The state would also pay the cost of those plans for individuals who lose their jobs.

"If you can't afford it, don't buy it," Democratic Gov. Chris Christie said at Friday's signing ceremony in Trenton.

The federal tax law eliminated the individual mandate last year. It was one of the most popular parts of President Barack Obama's law, and the elimination of it sparked concerns that insurance premiums were going too high.

Christie has previously stated his desire to repeal the individual mandate, but he had to back off after major health insurance companies announced plans to raise their rates by 20 percent.

The New Jersey proposal is the latest effort to address the problem of high health insurance costs.

In Oregon, the legislature passed a law requiring insurers to cover high-cost medications, including insulin, and the state is considering a bill that would force insurers to maintain a list of drugs that must be covered.

Luis Suarez was distraught following Liverpool’s draw with Crystal Palace on Monday, but the Uruguayan can take a measure of solace in the fact that he made history.

Suarez became the first player to score in two consecutive Premier League games since Alan Shearer in 1998 and the first since Frank Lampard against Manchester City in January 2002.

The 26-year-old also became the first player since Shearer to hit a hat-trick in consecutive Premier League appearances since Mark Hughes scored at Leeds United on 16 September 1999.

It was another frustrating afternoon for Liverpool manager Brendan Rodgers, who was unable to inspire his side to victory despite having the man advantage throughout.

However, the Reds did manage to keep a clean sheet for the fifth time in seven games – equalling their best run of the season.

Rodgers said: “Luis is delighted – it is fantastic for him. He worked so hard for this, to get two goals and two assists in two games.

“He has been absolutely brilliant this week and he deserves the credit. It was a great team performance.”

Rodgers was more concerned by the lack of a killer instinct in the final third, saying: “We were quite disappointed to concede late on.

“We could have played with a lot more intensity and it is disappointing not to come away with the three points.

Authorities in Bexar County, Texas, on Thursday responded to reports of three individuals with fatal gunshot wounds inside a gated community.

Bexar County Sheriff Javier Salazar said deputies and other emergency personnel arrived at the scene at 10:13 a.m. and found the victims, two men and a woman, dead in a home.

SALARZUAR: 'We cannot allow this...this is an ongoing investigation'

The sheriff said he could not identify the deceased or say whether they appeared to be one or more people. He said authorities believe the shooting was domestic, but did not provide further details.

"This is a very sad day here in Bexar County. We cannot allow this to happen," Salazar said during a news conference. "There are no words to describe the pain that our family is feeling today."

The sheriff said he had been receiving reports of a disturbance at a home on San Antonio Road in unincorporated Bexar County, about 30 miles northwest of downtown San Antonio.

SALARZUAR: 'It's a nightmare'

He said a 911 caller told dispatchers that three people were being shot.

College Basketball Power 10: Who Runs The Pac-12?
By Matthew Stevens | Senior Editor
It’s time for our second installment of the College Basketball Power 10, a ranking of the top ten teams in the Pac-12 Conference.
Our first edition was a success, so today’s rankings will be a bit different. While we expect that the Pac-12 will remain a league of powerhouses as it has been in recent years, this season’s competition is significantly more diverse than what we saw in 2016.
The conference was ranked No. 3 in last week’s ranking and could very well end up in the top five.
We’ll be breaking down the teams based on their strengths and weaknesses, as well as their chances of winning their respective conference tournaments.
While the Pac-12 has been known to run the table when it comes to the NCAA Tournament, this year’s conference tournament field looks a bit more difficult.
There are four teams that are considered co-favorite to win their respective conferences, while several others are legitimate title contenders.
